meta.title: Drift — Sculptural Sound Art
meta.description: Drift reimagines the white noise machine as sculptural sound art — exploring the history of sonic self-control from Buckwalter's first noise machine to the present, and asking what we lose when we mask the silence.

header.title: Drift
header.subtitle: Sculptural sound art exploring the continuum between noise and music.

hero.title: The white noise machine, reimagined as kinetic sound sculpture.
hero.introCopy: For decades, people have built technologies to control what they hear — white noise machines to sleep, nature recordings to focus, noise-cancelling headphones to tune out. Drift takes this impulse and makes it sculptural. Scaled larger than a human, these sculptures reimagine the white noise machine as an object you actively listen to rather than ignore. Using an Arduino microcontroller, servo motors, wood and tuned pipes, each sculpture slowly shifts from noise toward emergent musical patterns — inviting audiences to notice the sonic self-control they practice every day.

noteCards.title: What you'll experience
noteCards.body: Drift is sculptural as well as architectural — you don't just look at or listen to the work, you move around it. Enveloped in a dynamic soundscape among resonant materials, audiences hear themselves hearing, witnessing the transition from masking noise to emergent music.

noteCards.title: An interdisciplinary collaboration
noteCards.body: Sound design and programming by Tate Carson, design and fabrication by Tim Murray. The project integrates electronics and software with traditional woodworking.

noteCards.title: The central question
noteCards.body: What do we lose when we paper over silence? Drift asks audiences to sit with the sounds they usually use to mask the silence — and to notice what emerges.

process.title: How the work came together.
process.description: Drift evolved through repeated cycles of prototyping, material testing, and sonic calibration.

theory.title: How noise, sound, and listening shape daily life.
theory.description: Technologies like white noise machines modify perception and reshape mood and attention — what Mack Hagood calls orphic media — in ways we rarely examine. From Buckwalter's first white noise machine to Irv Teibel's Environments records to Bose's noise-cancelling headphones, people have spent decades engineering their own silence. Drift transforms that impulse into sculpture, making the pursuit of sonic control something you can see, hear, and stand beside. And by scaling the work beyond what can be experienced all at once, the installation creates a space audiences inhabit rather than observe.

cards.title: Noise as creative material
cards.body: The sculptures position noise not as something to mask or eliminate, but as creative material.

cards.title: What silence reveals
cards.body: Tinnitus is sometimes described as the brain's signal for silence — our attention straining to find meaning in the quiet. Drift asks a similar question: what emerges when we stop masking and start listening?

cards.title: Scale as confrontation
cards.body: Drift redesigns the white noise machine many times larger than its commercial counterpart, making it impractical for its intended purpose — masking unwanted sound in a living environment. This deliberate scaling transforms a background appliance into something you confront, stand beside, and reflect on. Drift allows us to hear ourselves hearing.

future.title: Four sculptures
future.body: Currently, Drift consists of a single kinetic sculpture. In its next phase, the installation will expand to four progressively smaller but identical sculptures — surrounding the listener in white noise and making the commentary on scale even more pronounced. Where one oversized machine already challenges the domestic comfort of its commercial counterpart, four will envelop the audience entirely, deepening the question of what happens when the tool we built to ignore sound becomes impossible to ignore.

quote.text: We spend our lives controlling what we hear — masking, cancelling, curating. Drift asks what happens when we stop managing the soundscape and start listening to it.

footer.text: Drift will debut at DSU Research Week, Dakota State University, Madison, SD. The project is supported by DSU and integrates sound art scholarship with innovative artistic methods.
